Nvidia, Salesforce Back Skild AI in $1.4 Billion Funding Round
Alphabet's Google Rolls Out US Beta of Gemini's Personal Intelligence Feature
OPEC's First Look at 2027 Points to Steady -2-
data.description:
ANZ Sees Gold Trading Above $5,000 in 2H on Geopolitical, Economic Uncertainty -- Market Talk

1256 GMT - Heightened geopolitical and economic uncertainty is expected to be a key driver of gold's performance this year, strengthening both safe-haven and diversification demand. "Geopolitical instability presents a compelling case for reserve diversification into gold," analysts at ANZ say. Meanwhile, concerns about the Federal Reserve's independence after U.S. federal prosecutors opened a criminal investigation into Chair Jerome Powell have unsettled investors and could potentially undermine confidence in U.S. assets. ANZ forecasts gold above $5,000 an ounce in the second half of the year, while flagging downside risks that include stronger-than-expected U.S. economic growth, a more hawkish Fed and easing geopolitical tensions. In afternoon trading, futures in New York rise 1% to $4,643.90 an ounce after hitting a high of $4,647.60.(giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Rises on Growing Fears of Supply Disruptions in Iran -- Market Talk

1254 GMT - Oil prices rise after opening the session lower, driven by mounting concerns about potential U.S. actions in Iran and the risk of supply disruptions in the region. Brent crude and WTI both rise 0.7% to $65.94 a barrel and $61.57 a barrel, respectively. "Investors' perceptions of the upside risks have increased," says Kieran Tompkins from Capital Economics. "The most high-impact risks to the global oil market stem from military action," including possible strikes against oil infrastructure or retaliation by Tehran that could disrupt shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, adverse weather, drone attacks and maintenance are threatening exports from Kazakhstan, with loadings at the Caspian Pipeline Consortium terminal falling to around 400,000 barrels a day in January from more than 1 million barrels a day in December, Kpler data shows. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

European Natural Gas Prices Hold Above 31 Euros on Cold Weather, Iran Supply Risks -- Market Talk

0916 GMT - European natural-gas prices slip in early trading but hold above 31 euros a megawatt hour on higher heating demand due to colder temperatures and fears that escalating tensions in Iran could disrupt LNG supplies. The benchmark Dutch TTF contract falls 1.4% to 31.03 euros but is up more than 10% on the week, having broken out of the 27 euros-30 euros trading range seen for several weeks. "A potential interruption of Iran's pipeline gas flows to Turkey could see European buyers buying more LNG just as demand in Asia is on the rise," ANZ analysts say. Meanwhile, Europe has seen accelerated withdrawals from its gas-storage facilities in recent days, with inventories slipping below 55%. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Precious Metals Hit Fresh Records on Softer Inflation, Haven Demand -- Market Talk

0904 GMT - Precious metals climb to fresh record highs, with silver breaking above $90 an ounce on softer-than-expected U.S. inflation data and rising geopolitical risks. In early trading, New York gold futures rise 0.9% to $4,642.60 a troy ounce after reaching a record of $4,647.60. Silver futures soar 4.2% to$90.02 an ounce, having hit a high of $91.37 earlier in the session. Traders' expectations for the Fed to cut rates by midyear got a boost from the latest CPI reading, benefiting nonyielding assets. "Persistent tensions, including political pressure on the Fed and geopolitical flashpoints, have lent further support to precious metals, with some possibility that silver and gold could reach new milestones in the coming months," says Soojin Kim from MUFG. "Markets are also watching a U.S. section 232 investigation that could lead to tariffs on silver, potentially tightening available inventories." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

Oil Falls After Settling 2.5% Higher With Venezuela, Iran in Focus -- Market Talk

0852 GMT - Oil prices slip in early trading after settling 2.5% higher in the previous session, as traders weigh a report that Venezuela has resumed crude exports under U.S. supervision and escalating unrest in Iran. Brent and WTI are both down 0.8% to $64.97 a barrel and $60.68 a barrel, respectively. Caracas has begun reversing production cuts made under a U.S. embargo and is instructing joint ventures in the country to resume output from well clusters, Reuters reported, citing unnamed sources. Meanwhile, "any disruption to Iran's oil industry threatens up to 3.5 million barrels a day of oil, of which nearly 2 million barrels a day reaches the international market," ANZ analysts say. Investors now await official Energy Information Administration figures due later on Wednesday following reports that crude stocks in the U.S. rose by 5.2 million barrels last week. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

--

London's Miners Rise as Precious Metals Climb -- Market Talk

0840 GMT - London's miners rise in opening trade as precious metals continue to climb. In New York, gold futures are up 0.75% at $4,634.10 a troy ounce while silver trades up at $89.63 an ounce. Both trade around record highs. The metals continue to be supported by safe-haven demand due to investor fears over the independence of the U.S. Federal Reserve. In London, commodity giant Glencore rises 1.5% while Rio Tinto is up 0.4%. Precious metal miner Endeavour is up 2.3% while Hochschild Mining and Fresnillo are both up around 1%.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

--

Gold and Silver Hit Fresh Highs Amid Geopolitical Risks -- Market Talk

0514 GMT - Gold and silver hit fresh record highs in the Asian session amid rising geopolitical risks, which typically enhance the safe-haven appeal of precious metals. President Trump on Tuesday told Iranians protesting against their government that "help is on its way," a sign that the U.S. might soon take action against the regime. Highly fluid developments in Iran underscore the persistence of geopolitical uncertainty, according to two FX strategists at OCBC Group Research in a research report. The fundamental story for precious metals remains intact," they add. Spot gold is up 1.1% at $4,634.75/oz after touching $4,639.64/oz, and spot silver climbs 4.5% to $90.84/oz after hitting $91.55/oz, ICE data show. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

--

Iron Ore Futures Gain Amid Lifted Sentiment -- Market Talk

0224 GMT - Iron ore futures are higher in the Asian session, as China pledges to roll out a package of policies to spur domestic consumption, Commonwealth Bank of Australia economists write in a recent note. This likely lifts sentiment, supporting iron ore prices, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange rose 0.2% to 822.0 yuan a metric ton. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

--

Upside for Crude Palm Oil Fades as Indonesia Maintains Biofuel Mandate -- Market Talk

0128 GMT - Upside catalysts for crude palm oil have likely disappeared after Indonesia decided to maintain its current B40 policy--which mandates a 40% palm-based biodiesel blend--in 2026, with B50 biofuel mandate kept as a contingency for 2H, CIMB Securities analyst Ivy Ng Lee Fang says in a note. This decision reduces expectations for a near-term boost in palm oil demand, as B50 could have added up to 3 million tons of demand. Indonesia's plans to raise palm oil export levies, which could pressure domestic CPO prices further, are also weighing on the outlook, she says. Policy risks have also increased with Indonesia planning to seize an additional 4-5 million hectares of plantations in 2026, adding uncertainty for producers with significant exposure to Indonesia, she adds. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

--

Copper Rises; Pullbacks Should Be Capped -- Market Talk

0120 GMT - Copper rises in early Asia trade, with the three-month LME copper contract 0.4% higher at $13,217.00 a ton. Spreads remain tight, with the premium for cash copper reaching $63/ton over the three-month contract, Sucden Financial analysts Daria Efanova and Viktoria Kuszak say in a note. That's cushioning downside risks, offering better support for copper compared with the rest of the base metals complex. As long as spreads remain tight, pullbacks in copper prices are expected to stay limited, the analysts add. (jason.chau@wsj.com)


Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-14-26 1206ET

data.title: OPEC's First Look at 2027 Points to Steady Oil-Demand Growth -- Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

-- Brent crude oil rises 0.7% to $65.90 a barrel.

-- European benchmark gas is up 1.7% to 32.03 euros a megawatt-hour.

-- Gold futures are up 0.5% to $4,624.10 a troy ounce.

-- LME three-month copper futures are up 0.4% to $13,206.50 a metric ton.


TOP STORY:

OPEC's First Look at 2027 Points to Steady Oil-Demand Growth

The Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries expects world oil demand to grow at a steady pace in 2027 in its first outlook for the year.

Demand is forecast to rise by 1.34 million barrels a day next year--slightly below 2026's estimated 1.38 million barrels a day--supported by a faster global economic growth as easing trade tensions, supportive fiscal measures and monetary policy lift activity in major economies, the group said in its closely watched monthly report.

The Vienna-based cartel and its allies are navigating a series of challenges--from growing unrest in Iran and the U.S. capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, to market forecasts of a looming supply glut.


OTHER STORIES:

BP Flags $5 Billion Write-Down in Low-Carbon Segment, Warns of Weak Oil-Trading

BP flagged a write-down of up to $5 billion in its gas and low-carbon energy segment and warned its oil-trading performance would be weak, as the latest energy major to forecast an earnings hit to cap a tough year for the industry.

The London-based energy company is in the early stages of a turnaround intended to reverse falling profits and boost a share price that fell under pressure after its strategic misstep into low-carbon energy.

--

U.S. Ethanol Output Smashes Previous Records

The Energy Information Administration reported that average ethanol production in the U.S. jumped by nearly 100,000 barrels a day in the past week, leading to an average well above any previous record high.

In its weekly report published Wednesday, the EIA said that average daily ethanol production for the week ended Jan. 9 was 1.196 million barrels a day. Not only is that above the high end of estimates from analysts surveyed by Dow Jones this week, but it's well above the prior record high of 1.131 million barrels a day set last month.

--

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Post Weekly Increase

U.S. crude oil inventories increased last week as imports rose, and gasoline stocks posted a ninth straight weekly build, according to data released Wednesday by the U.S. Energy Information Administration.

Commercial crude oil stocks excluding the Strategic Petroleum Reserve rose by 3.4 million barrels to 422.4 million barrels in the week ended Jan. 9, and were about 3% below the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said. Crude stocks were expected to have fallen by 1.4 million barrels, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts.

--

Triple Flag Precious Metals Sees 4Q Revenue Growth On Higher Precious Metals Prices

Triple Flag Precious Metals reported preliminary fourth?quarter results showing revenue growth, supported by higher gold and silver prices.

The precious metals miner said Wednesday that revenue is expected to come in at $118.9 million, up from $74.2 million. According to FactSet, forecasts were for revenue of $121.6 million.


MARKET TALKS:

Canadian Lumber Producers Seen Rebounding in 2026 -- Market Talk

1134 ET - Canadian lumber producers are set up for a stronger 2026, according to Raymond James analyst Daryl Swetlishoff, who says the sector has finally hit bottom after a four-year downturn. With valuations at historic lows and supply tightening due to mill curtailments, restricted Canadian harvesting and limited U.S. imports, the analyst sees the risk-reward profile shifting to the upside. Potential U.S. housing policy changes ahead could also improve affordability and lift lumber demand. Depressed share prices may also encourage consolidation in the industry as cash-rich buyers look for deals. Against this backdrop, Swetlishoff picks Canfor and Interfor as top winners with strong buy ratings, while West Fraser is upgraded to outperform from market perform as conditions across the industry begin to improve. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

--

Russian Wheat Exports Seen Higher in January -- Market Talk

1108 ET - More wheat is seen being shipped out of Russia in January, according to agricultural research firm SovEcon in a note. SovEcon estimates that Russia will ship anywhere from 3.0 to 3.4 million metric tons of wheat this month, which is a sizable increase from the 2.3 million tons of wheat exported for the month last year. But the firm also notes that the five-year average of Russian wheat exports is 3.1 million tons, within the range of SovEcon's current estimate. The firm says that the export pace has picked up as major importers like Egypt are seeking to buy more, and margins for Russian exports are improving. CBOT wheat futures are down 0.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Cattle Turns Lower But Further Gains Possible -- Market Talk

1049 ET - Live cattle futures on the CME are down 0.5% in morning trade, as traders hit the brakes on gains seen so far this week. But the overall gradual up-trend for cattle futures remains intact, with the contract gaining 3% in the past 30 days. More buying may be coming from technical traders, says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. However, a fundamental easing of the tight supply and demand picture seen last year looks to curb whatever upside may be seen in the coming days and weeks, Blohm adds. January's WASDE report showed higher-weight cattle driving increased U.S. beef production, with lower exports and higher imports. Lean hog futures rise 0.3%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Grain Futures Attempt Turnaround -- Market Talk

0957 ET - CBOT grains mostly rise in early trading, with prices attempting a turnaround after being throttled by higher-than-expected production estimates out of the JanuaryWASDE report. Supporting today's gain is chatter about buyers taking advantage of the lower prices by buying larger quantities of corn and soybeans. "Exports were already hot and end users are loading up further if possible," says Matt Zeller of StoneX in a note. On the export front, the USDA announced new flash sales of U.S. corn and soybeans, with 136,000 metric tons of corn sold to South Korea for delivery in the 2025/26 marketing year and 334,000 tons of soybeans sold to China for 2025/26 delivery. CBOT corn rises 0.7% and soybeans climb 0.7%. Wheat falls 0.1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

Brazil's Petrobras Faces Political Risk But Pays High Dividend Yield -- Market Talk

0946 ET - Brazil's state-controlled oil major Petrobras faces political turbulence, UBS BB analysts write, as they trim the price target while maintaining a buy rating. They expect the stock to pay a dividend yield between 10% and 11% this year, which would be at the high end of their forecast for global peers. "Only Shell has a similar yield," they say. UBS BB expects "solid production levels." Presidential elections in October could have a negative impact on Petrobras' investment plans. A substantial decline in crude prices could also threaten capex. UBS BB reduced the price target to 40 from 42 Brazilian reais.(paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

--

Aluminum Prices Likely to Stay Elevated -- Market Talk

0930 ET - Prices for aluminum are at levels last seen in the early days of the Russian-Ukraine war in 2022. Driving this gain are projections for a global aluminum deficit in 2026, as the excess manufacturing capacity China has had for aluminum over the past 20 years may finally be used up. Aluminum prices were mostly low during that time of Chinese domination, which in turn discouraged the creation of new production. Citi Research updates its outlook for aluminum prices over the next three months to $3,400 a metric ton, with uncertainty remaining about the health of the Chinese manufacturing sector longer-term. The 90-day forward contract on the LME is down 0.1% currently, to $3,192.40 a ton. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

--

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Give Back Gains -- Market Talk

0904 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are returning gains from the previous two sessions, as the colder weather outlook for the second half of January is seen unlikely to keep the storage surplus from building. "Rising storage surpluses despite a cold back half of January may give the impression of a fundamentally oversupplied supply/demand balance to offer bearish risks later this month," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. Nymex natural gas is off 5.1% at $3.245/mmBtu.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

US-Canada Trade Policy Shift Seen as Key to Canada's Steel Outlook -- Market Talk

0847 ET - Canada's steel sector will hinge on the structural reset in trade policy now taking shape between U.S. and Canada, says RBC analyst James McGarragle. In a report, the analyst says that the real turning point will come from CUSMA-related tariff relief expected in the latter half of the year, which could narrow Canada's steep price discount to the U.S., estimated at about 40% currently. McGarragle says recent Canadian government protection measures are already helping, noting that they "should help stabilize the Canadian steel market in the near term."(adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

--

Oil Extends Rally on Geopolitical Risk Premium -- Market Talk

0834 ET - Crude futures press higher with geopolitical risks outweighing the market's broader global oversupply concerns. Citi raises its 0-3 month Brent price target to $70 citing supply disruption risks and possible increases in geopolitical premium related to Iran and Russia/Ukraine. "We now think this oil rally has room to extend above our $55-$65 a barrel forecast range," Francesco Martoccia says in a note. Protests in Iran have been mostly away from the main oil producing areas, so "current risks are skewed toward political and logistical frictions rather than direct outages," he says. WTI is up 1.1% at $61.82 a barrel and Brent is up 1.2% at $66.25. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

--

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

01-14-26 1206ET

Honda Motor Co., Ltd. Begins U.S. Production of New Lineup of ProZision Battery-Powered Lawn Mowers
data.description:
Honda North Carolina Manufacturing (NCM) announced the start of production of its first battery-powered zero-turn radius (ZTR) mowers at its facility in Swepsonville, N.C. ProZision ZTR mowers are produced using domestic and globally made parts and will be available at specialty equipment dealers beginning in February 2026. The Honda ProZision ZTR series represents the first Honda riding mowers for commercial landscaping, built in the same facility that began producing walk- behind lawn mowers more than 40 years ago. Honda associates at the nearly 700,000-square-foot NCM facility have demonstrated incredible flexibility since starting with production of lawnmowers in 1984.

NCM has now reached cumulative production of over 50 million products, including a variety of power equipment products, such as snow blowers, string trimmers, mini-tillers, water pumps, generators, general-purpose engines and, in 2023, the addition of all-terrain vehicles (ATVs). The all-new product family of ZTR mowers includes 54-inch and 60-inch models designed for professional-level durability, comfort, and cut quality, powered by five 48-volt brushless electric motors designed to stand up to the rigors of commercial use. In the future, production of the ProZision ZTR 54- and 60-inch battery-powered mowers will include parts made in the Swepsonville facility's new plastics department, which was part of a $9.7 million investment announced last fall.

Skild AI, Inc. announced that it has received $1.4 billion in funding from a group of investors
data.description: Skild AI, Inc. announced that it has received close to $1.4 billion in a round of funding led by returning investor, SoftBank Group Corp. on January 14, 2026. The transaction include participation from new investors, Destiny Capital Corporation, Alpha Square Group Inc., KIC Ventures, LLC, PA Capital Management, LLC, Andra Capital LLC, TF Capital, Salesforce Ventures, LLC, CommonSpirit Health, Schneider Electric S.E., 1789 Capital Management, LLC, Disruptive AI Venture Capital, NVentures LLC, Macquarie Group Limited, returning investors, Mirae Asset Securities Co., Ltd., LG Technology Ventures USA, Inc., SoftBank Group Corp., Bezos Expeditions, LLC, Lightspeed Ventures, LLC, Coatue Management, L.L.C., Felicis Ventures Management Company, LLC, Sequoia Capital Operations LLC and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. The company issued convertible preferred shares in the transaction.

data.title: Giulio Terzariol Appointed General Manager and Deputy CEO of Generali
data.description:
(Alliance News) - Assicurazioni Generali Spa announced on Wednesday that Giulio Terzariol has taken on the role of general manager and deputy CEO.

The appointment had been approved by the board of directors last November.

Generali shares closed Wednesday up 0.4% at EUR34.81 per share.

By Giuseppe Fabio Ciccomascolo, Alliance News senior reporter

Comments and questions to redazione@alliancenews.com

ICE Midday : Canola Takes a Step Back
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange were slightly lower in the middle of Wednesday trading amid a weaker Canadian dollar and mixed sentiment in comparable oils.

Chicago soyoil was relatively steady, while European rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil were down. Crude oil made gains as tensions escalate in Iran.

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney landed in China Wednesday where he and Chinese President Xi Jinping are expected to discuss trade matters, including China's tariffs on Canadian canola. However, one analyst said he "doesn't see something positive out of this." The Canadian dollar was up less than one-tenth of a U.S. cent compared with Tuesday's close.

About 35,700 canola contracts have traded at 11:15 a.m. ET. Prices in Canadian dollars per metric tonne: 


Canola 
           Price      Change 
Mar       628.10     dn 4.70 
May       636.70     dn 5.20 
Jul       643.70     dn 4.80 
Nov       640.60     dn 4.30

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

01-14-26 1147ET

Goolsbee calls Powell 'first-ballot hall-of-fame Fed chair,' NPR says
data.description:
Jan 14 (Reuters) - The independence of the U.S. central bank is critical to containing inflation, and attacks against it, such as the Trump administration's criminal probe into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, threaten to make that task harder, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee said on Wednesday.

"The independence of the Fed couldn't be more important for the long-run inflation rate in this country," Goolsbee told National Public Radio. "Any place where you don't have central bank independence, inflation comes roaring back."

"We've spent the last five years fighting to get the inflation rate down - and that hasn't been easy - and if you're attacking the independence of the Fed, that makes that problem worse," Goolsbee said.

Goolsbee's comments follow moves over the weekend by the U.S. Justice Department to issue subpoenas to the Fed and Powell over comments he made last summer to the Senate regarding cost overruns at a $2.5 billion building renovation project at the central bank's headquarters complex in Washington.

Powell, in a video statement on Sunday, called the unprecedented action a pretext and said the administration's threats have emerged following President Donald Trump's demands for sharply lower interest rates.

The administration's maneuvers sparked global condemnation by other major central banks and led some Republican U.S. senators to threaten to hold up any nominee Trump puts forward as a successor to Powell - whose term as Fed leader expires in May - while any Justice Department investigation is underway.

"I consider Chair Powell to be a first-ballot hall-of-fame Fed chair," Goolsbee said. "And if we're going to get into a circumstance where the independence - or even the integrity - of Chair Powell is in question, we're in a bad spot."

(Reporting by Dan Burns; Editing by Paul Simao)
